Hey, it's Andre and Sean today. We are talking about the customer onboarding series. This is a 10,000 foot view. I'll do a quick narration of what we see on screen. And then I'll hand it over to Andre and we'll just kind of do what we've been doing all along, which is ping pong back and forth and see where the conversation leads. So see two variations here. Don't think of either of these as prescriptive like you should do this. Your situation will vary. Your needs will vary. This is just two, sort of showing two different approaches. So there's customer onboarding series A at the top of the yellow. That's just set up three weeks, daily email, start to finish. You know, the use case for this, I mean, you can imagine it could be anything. It could be a digital course. It's something where it makes sense for somebody to get a daily email over a significant period of time. If you had, for example, a three week course or, you know, some training or something like that, this would make a lot of sense. For each day, you could be calling attention to something that the person should be paying attention to, for example. You know, in the bottom one is a different variation, which you may have, and this is the customer onboarding series B, you might have daily emails for a period of time. Someone first starts out, I'll use the course example again. Someone first starts out and there are, you know, you're blocking them, taking them by the hand through the first two weeks each day. And then that transitions after that initial onboarding and maybe they get a weekly email over some period of time, this example, six weeks. Again, neither of these are prescriptive. They're just there to show that really sky is the limit. And the biggest challenge, and I'm going to set this up perfectly for myself, which is I'm going to tell you the biggest challenge of a customer onboarding series and then I'm going to hand it off to Andre to answer. Like what we do with this biggest challenge. So the biggest challenge with an onboarding series is that there is no, there's no right answer. Right, there's no, if you have a physical product, for example, that you ship out and you want to tell the story of like how it was made and the attention to detail and that that that creates its own set of requirements. You can have a digital course and that course could be short or it could be long or you could have some sort of membership continuity where there's a course plus a community after that. So maybe initially there's lots of onboarding and then there's periodic communication and there's no right answer. And because of that, it's very difficult to say this is how to create your customer onboarding series. So with all of that difficulty identified, I'm just going to hand it off to Andre and have him detangle all that difficulty for you. Sorry. Well, OK, so one thing should be obvious by looking at this and comparing it to to an obvious to a customer relation building series and SOS is that these these are typically longer. And in every case that I can remember, they can be significantly longer. And there's, you know, there's there's many reasons for that. One of which is, you know, someone's just made a significant investment. They've now been our customer and this is an opportunity to to really delight them. And and this is the perfect mechanism to be able to do that. So I'll run through a few scenarios or use cases that that that we've done in the past. And this also preceded Sean showing up. So, for example, all this one, a man is version two. So the second edition of that many years ago, over a decade ago, is the entire product was delivered as emails. So when somebody made a purchase and they got the the COS, this was me delivering the product. And that happened over six weeks. So it was six weeks of emails and email every single day. So, you know, so I kind of got two things crammed into one. So it was the onboarding series as well as me delivering a product. And there were no content pages that the emails were was the content. Then when I released, or respond to madness three, so the third edition a few years later, the course itself was delivered on the website as all of our courses currently are. And I then made this onboarding series here, an extension of the product. So it's it's it's served to reinforce certain things that were taught inside of the product. But there was there was stuff inside of here that ideas ideas, stories, things that were told in a in a in a slightly different point of view. As part of this, the sequence that didn't exist in the main content course. So if somebody didn't receive this, there would be, you know, there was there would have been a whole bunch of value that they wouldn't have been able to to to see and and and and experience. So one of the things that we like to do is is is turn these series into an extension of the product that they've just purchased. And it's a wonderful way of really demonstrating value is in that version. And so this bottom version here is a really nice way of of doing that where you have these these daily emails that can last for as long as they need to last for. You know, in this case, two weeks, it could be three weeks, could be even longer than that. And then at some inflection point, you can do these weekly check ins that can either be ongoing. You just keep keep dumping them on your and and every single person, every customer that gets to the point. So let's say this is the this is the endpoint and then you add another one. Well, everybody's going to get that as soon as they've reached this point. But that's not a requirement. That's that's just a tweak and extra version. But yeah, so these are just variations of of doing this. And this, as Sean said, there's no rather rather wrong way, better or worse way. It's just an opportunity to demonstrate value in this in this format that is very, very effective at at building a relationship with somebody that's just raised their hand. And save you some money. And that's it's so interesting how easy this is to overlook to we've all experienced it, which is, you know, it's like we do so much work to get a customer. And then you get the customary like, great. And then you go back to trying to get customers. It's like, wait a minute. Someone just did the thing you've been trying to get them to do for days, weeks, years. Like, let's not let's not discount how important that is. I'll draw attention to a couple of things too that just jumped out of me when you were when you were speaking I mean one first and foremost, the principles of serialized narrative apply to a customer onboarding series just like everything else. This is not. This is not just functional. Yes, parts of it are if it's a digital course for example you need to send credentials and you need. You need to send receipts and that's short all of that just functional, but we know that people buy things and experience things for other reasons besides functional reasons for emotional reason reasons. Social reasons, there's there's a whole list of reasons why people buy things and that doesn't stop. Once they've made the purchase. So, to acknowledge that someone is taking a risk, they've given you some money, taken a big risk and taken a step toward you to take a step back, and then really appreciate that, and to recognize that and to say, I'm going to take the time to walk you through what to expect to fill in the blanks like that's that's the value of the customer onboarding series and it could be for, you know I'm thinking of when I purchased something a physical product recently. I began to before I even received it I began to get emails that were creating excitement about what I should expect how to set it up. Why the technology works the way that it does like all of that happened before the thing even arrived, and I was excited when it arrived so this is not limited to the digital world only this absolutely apply and if you sell a widget, and people know what the widget is, and I think it's a customer onboarding series to explain what the red widget does. So one of the things I've done in the past, and we will do over here is we will theme some of these emails so for example, these first three in this example would talk about, or draw people's attention to the strategic upstream actions will will paint a different gloss on it and a little different perspective that's not in the course. It's just said slightly differently. And then we'll move on to to the next, maybe theme of three emails which would be the obvious for example and then there'll be the sos and then there'll be the revenue engine, the components of the revenue engine. I think that's a great explanation of the customer onboarding series. I mean it really captures there's so much nuance in this and really for anybody listening to this. Taking a step back and just asking yourself what if nothing else what conversation would you have with somebody. After they've made the purchase and what would you tell them at first and the logistical stuff you would cover yours. You should have received the receipt and you get your login and you would say that but then you would move on like you know the first thing. Whatever they're going to be exposed to first you would talk about that and you would add some nuance and maybe draw attention to some different parts as they went through. I mean there's so many ways that this can work really well and it's so easy to forget. And I think that's one of the that's the critical lesson I'll make from my last point is this is something that's incredibly easy to forget yet it's also incredibly powerful. Functionally, if you want a reason to do this that's just purely functional. It will reduce refund rates I mean that's it's a functional reason to do this because you'll increase customer satisfaction. But beyond that you're going to make happier customers and we have defined our business as we're in the happy customer business. And then we know this makes happy customers and we know that having happy customers is valuable on its own. And the customer onboarding series is an excellent way to increase the likelihood that you're making happy customers not just customers. Yeah, anything else to add before we wrap this one up.
